rationale: The citing paper uses Novemsky et al. (2007) as a core conceptual and empirical basis for its theory of preference fluency in choice. It explicitly adopts their definition of preference fluency and cites their finding that fluency can affect compromise-option selection when developing hypotheses. The paper then builds on that base by introducing private self-awareness as an antecedent of preference fluency and testing resulting effects on compromise and variety-seeking choices.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Novemsky et al. (2007) by taking preference fluency as the underlying mechanism and examining a new driver, private self-awareness, plus boundary conditions for when fluency-based effects on choice appear.
